title: India's Cold Protocol: How the Atwal Incident Exposed New Delhi's Khalistan Tolerance Line
lede: Justin Trudeau's February 2018 India visit became a test case for how far New Delhi will compromise diplomatic courtesy when faced with what it views as Canadian complicity in separatist activism.

sections.context: Trudeau's week-long visit to India arrived at a moment of considerable friction between Delhi and Ottawa that had accumulated largely out of public view. The presence of Jaspal Atwal, a Canadian citizen convicted of attempting to assassinate an Indian minister in 1986, at two official state functions—including a reception at the Canadian High Commission in Delhi—signaled to the Indian government something New Delhi had grown increasingly concerned about: Canada's perceived unwillingness or inability to regulate pro-Khalistan political activity on its soil. Atwal's attendance was not incidental protocol error but a flashpoint that exposed deeper structural mistrust. The Indian government's response was deliberately calibrated: Modi did not receive Trudeau at the airport, a significant departure from standard protocol for a visiting PM of a major democratic nation. The visit proceeded at markedly reduced ceremonial levels, signaling official displeasure without rupturing formal relations. This was not mere optics. By downgrading the protocol posture, India was communicating to Ottawa that diplomatic hospitality is conditional on demonstrated commitment to managing actors New Delhi classifies as threats to its sovereignty and territorial integrity. The visit highlighted an asymmetry in how the two countries viewed the same actors: what Canada treated as protected political speech and community activism, India treated as active separatism enabled by state tolerance.
sections.threads: The Atwal incident sits within a longer trajectory of Canadian-Indian tension over Khalistan-linked politics that extends back decades but has sharpened considerably since [[india-canada-punjab-separatism-1990s|the 1990s Punjab crisis]]. During that period, Canada became home to significant Sikh diaspora populations, some of whom maintained activist networks focused on Khalistan independence. By the early 2010s, India had begun to perceive a pattern: Canadian political leaders maintaining or cultivating relationships with Canada-based Khalistan advocacy groups, Canadian media providing platforms for narratives critical of the Indian state's handling of Punjab, and most troublingly from Delhi's perspective, Canadian officials extending diplomatic courtesies to individuals with documented separatist backgrounds. The Trudeau government's explicit multiculturalism doctrine, while domestically popular, was read by Indian analysts as creating permissive conditions for anti-India activism. This visit also needs to be understood in parallel with [[india-canada-trade-friction-2017-2018|concurrent disputes over agricultural trade and dairy tariffs]], which had already strained the bilateral relationship and limited the political capital Trudeau could draw upon to smooth over the Atwal episode. More broadly, the incident reflected India's growing strategic confidence in asserting its own red lines regardless of democratic partner sensitivities—a posture evident in [[india-pakistan-diplomatic-escalation-2016-2019|India's hardening stance on Pakistan-linked terrorism]] during the same period. Canada, by contrast, was learning that its traditional liberal tolerance for diaspora political expression was coming into conflict with a rising power's non-negotiable sovereignty concerns.
sections.signal: Analysts should monitor three specific indicators in the Canada-India relationship over the next 18 months. First, track whether Canada initiates formal legal proceedings or deportation actions against [[khalistan-activists-canada-watch|pro-Khalistan activists with documented security concerns]], particularly those appearing at diplomatic events or receiving media platforms. Such action would signal Ottawa capitulating to Indian pressure; its absence would suggest the relationship enters a period of managed coldness. Second, observe whether the Indian government establishes new formal mechanisms for vetting guests at diplomatic functions involving Canadian officials, which would indicate institutionalization of the mistrust rather than a one-off response. Third, watch for either reciprocal protocol downgrading when Canadian officials visit India—further receptions at lower levels, reduced visibility of Indian counterparts—or conversely, a gradual return to normal ceremonial standards, which would suggest either entrenchment of tension or quiet resolution through backstop diplomatic channels. The critical variable is whether India perceives measurable policy change in Ottawa's approach to Khalistan advocacy, or whether it concludes that such change is impossible within Canada's constitutional framework. That determination will shape whether this is a temporary diplomatic friction or the beginning of structural decoupling on a key South Asian relationship.
